did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a deep learning-based decision system for automatic diagnosis of skin lesions (melanoma vs. benign nevus). The methods included a custom neural network, three fine-tuned Convolutional Neural Networks (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large) using transfer learning, and a feature-based method with a Support Vector Machine (SVM). These AI models were trained and tested on the PH2 and ISIC 2019 dermoscopic image databases to obtain classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating a deep learning-based decision system for automatic diagnosis of skin lesions. It details the creation of a custom neural network, fine-tuning of three CNNs (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and a feature-based SVM method. The authors report new results from experiments conducted on the PH2 and ISIC 2019 databases, including specific accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and Dice coefficient metrics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning–Based Methods for Automatic Diagnosis of Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based decision system for the automatic diagnosis of skin lesions, specifically differentiating melanoma from benign nevi. The proposed method integrates a custom neural network, three fine-tuned convolutional neural networks (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and a feature-based support vector machine into a global fusion-based decision system. These models were trained and evaluated using dermoscopic images from the PH2 and ISIC 2019 databases. The evaluation approach involved calculating biostatistic parameters such as accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and Dice similarity coefficient for both individual classifiers and the fused system. The results demonstrated that the proposed fusion system achieved higher accuracy than individual methods, reaching 95% on the PH2 database and 93% on the ISIC 2019 database. The study concludes that combining multiple classifiers with accuracy-based weighting improves the performance of automated melanoma detection systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer. Used as a positive class in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. benign nevus) on the PH2 and ISIC 2019 databases. 40 images from PH2 and 80 images from ISIC 2019 were used.

Benign melanocytic lesion. Used as the negative class in the binary classification task. 60 images of common nevi from PH2 and 120 images of common nevi from ISIC 2019 were used. Note: The PH2 database also contained atypical nevi, but the study selected only common nevi for the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (melanoma vs. benign nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a decision system for automatic diagnosis of skin lesions, specifically differentiating melanoma from benign nevi. The methods include a custom neural network, three fine-tuned CNNs (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and a feature-based SVM. The models predict discrete classes (melanoma or benign nevus) using confusion matrices, accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity metrics. The fusion system combines these binary classification outputs.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database acquired at the Dermatology department of Hospital Pedro Hispano (Matosinhos, Portugal) for research purposes. It contains images acquired using a magnification of ×20 under conditions similar to the Tuebinger Mole Analyzer system. The full database consists of 200 images (80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanomas) with resolution 768 × 560 pixels. This study selected a subset of 100 images.

use.training: Used for training the custom neural network, fine-tuning CNNs (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and training the feature-based SVM method. For CNNs, 70% of the selected images were used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. For CNNs, 30% of the selected images were used for validation. The custom NN and feature-based method results are reported on the validation phase.
use.testing: Used for testing the global fusion-based decision system.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) collection, which includes databases BCN_20000, HAM 10000, and MSK. It contains 25,332 JPEG images of skin lesions including nevi, vascular lesions, squamous cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, solar lentigo, and seborrheic keratosis. This study selected a specific subset of 200 images.

use.training: Used for training the custom neural network, fine-tuning CNNs (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and training the feature-based SVM method. For CNNs, 70% of the selected images were used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. For CNNs, 30% of the selected images were used for validation. The custom NN and feature-based method results are reported on the validation phase.
use.testing: Used for testing the global fusion-based decision system.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and Dice Similarity Coefficient for individual classifiers and the fusion system.
To evaluate the validity of the diagnostic test by measuring the area under the curve for training, validation, and testing phases.
To analyze the training performance and convergence of the neural network during the training process.
To monitor the network performance and determine when to stop training based on the gradient approaching zero.
To determine the fusion weights for the global fusion-based decision system by assigning weights to individual classifiers based on their calculated accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, Dice Similarity Coefficient, and ROC curves without statistical significance testing.

Custom Neural Network.notes: Testing phase accuracy was not explicitly stated as a single percentage in the text, but confusion matrix details indicated 2 Me classified as Cn and 3 Cn classified as Me out of 100 images. Best validation performance MSE was 0.1789 at epoch 27. Lowest gradient value was 0.0013104 at epoch 33.

GoogleNet CNN.notes: Training time: 1 min 45 s. Epochs: 6. Minibatch size: 1. Weight learning rate factor: 20. Bias learn rate factor: 20.

ResNet-101 CNN.notes: Training time: 6 min 7 s. Epochs: 6. Minibatch size: 10. Weight learning rate factor: 10. Bias learn rate factor: 10.

NasNet-Large CNN.notes: Training time: 748 min 59 s. Max iterations: 1854.

Feature-Based Method (SVM).notes: Best shape information achieved with 4x4 cell size.

Global Fusion System.notes: Fusion weights established based on validation accuracies of individual classifiers. Threshold factor of 0.7 was chosen experimentally.

Custom Neural Network.notes: Testing phase accuracy was 89%. Best validation performance MSE was 0.484 at epoch 26. Training time: 150 min.

GoogleNet CNN.notes: Training time: 1 min 46 s. Minibatch size: 10. Weight learning rate factor: 10. Bias learn rate factor: 10.

ResNet-101 CNN.notes: Confusion matrix details: 3 Cn misclassified as Me, 4 Me misclassified as Cn.

NasNet-Large CNN.notes: Training time: 748 min 59 s. Max iterations: 1854.

Feature-Based Method (SVM).notes: Best shape information achieved with 4x4 cell size.

Global Fusion System.notes: Fusion weights established based on validation accuracies of individual classifiers. Threshold factor of 0.7 was chosen experimentally.

architecture_used.main_model: Global fusion-based decision system integrating a custom neural network, three fine-tuned Convolutional Neural Networks (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and a feature-based Support Vector Machine (SVM).
architecture_used.pretraining: GoogleNet, ResNet-101, and NasNet-Large were pre-trained on large ImageNet and Places365 databases. The custom neural network and SVM were trained from scratch on the target datasets.
architecture_used.input_size: GoogleNet, ResNet-101: 224 × 224 × 3 pixels. NasNet-Large: 331 × 331 × 3 pixels. Custom NN and SVM: Resized to match learning step dimensions (specific pixel dimensions not explicitly stated for these, but resizing was performed).

custom_neural_network.layers: Input layer, Output layer (2 targets: common nevus, melanoma), and variable hidden layers (72 layers for PH2 database, 400 layers for ISIC 2019 database).

google_net.implementation: MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox (pre-trained on Places365)

resnet_101.implementation: MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox (pre-trained on ImageNet)

nasnet_large.implementation: MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox (pre-trained on ImageNet)

svm.features: Extracted from images using feature-based method (shape information with 4x4 cell size, grayscale conversion, adaptive thresholding/binarization).

training_details.fine_tuning_method: Transfer learning. Last two layers of CNNs replaced with new fully connected layers (2 outputs). Previous layers frozen (learning rates set to zero) to avoid overfitting and reduce time. New layers had faster learning rates.
training_details.data_augmentation: Used for training images via augmented image datastore to avoid overfitting. Not used for validation images.

classification_or_output_head.cnn_heads: Last two layers replaced with new layers fitted to images. Final classification layer and last learnable layer use features extracted by convolutional layers to combine into loss value, class probabilities, and predicted label. Output classes: common nevus and melanoma.
classification_or_output_head.svm_head: Classification based on extracted features (shape information).
classification_or_output_head.custom_nn_head: Output layer with 2 targets (common nevi and melanoma).
classification_or_output_head.fusion_strategy: Global fusion-based decision system using accuracy-based weighting. Fusion weights established based on validation accuracies of individual classifiers. Threshold factor of 0.7 chosen experimentally.

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using the DullRazor algorithm (generalized grayscale morphological closing, shape verification, bilinear interpolation, adaptive median filtering)
Removal of dermatologist signs/markers near the lesion (performed by DullRazor)
Image resizing to match network input requirements (224 × 224 × 3 pixels for GoogleNet and ResNet-101; 331 × 331 × 3 pixels for NasNet-Large)
Grayscale conversion (for the custom neural network method)
Data augmentation (applied to training images via augmented image datastore to avoid overfitting; not applied to validation images)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for training images via an augmented image datastore to automatically resize images to match network input requirements (224x224x3 for GoogleNet/ResNet-101, 331x331x3 for NasNet-Large) and to avoid overfitting. Not applied to validation images.

conclusions: The proposed global fusion-based decision system, which integrates a custom neural network, three fine-tuned convolutional neural networks (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large), and a feature-based support vector machine, achieves higher accuracy than individual methods for automatic diagnosis of skin lesions.
The fusion system utilizes accuracy-based weighting to combine the outputs of individual classifiers, establishing fusion weights based on the validation accuracy of each method.

On the PH2 database, the global fusion system achieved an accuracy of 95%, outperforming individual classifiers such as the custom neural network (93.33% validation accuracy) and the CNNs (90% validation accuracy).

On the ISIC 2019 database, the global fusion system achieved an accuracy of 93%, outperforming individual classifiers such as the custom neural network (88.33% validation accuracy) and the CNNs (ranging from 88.33% to 91.66% validation accuracy).
The study demonstrates that combining multiple classifiers with accuracy-based weighting improves the performance of automated melanoma detection systems.

The custom neural network required 72 hidden layers for the PH2 database and 400 hidden layers for the ISIC 2019 database to achieve optimal results, indicating that network complexity may need to scale with dataset size.

Transfer learning using pre-trained models (GoogleNet, ResNet-101, NasNet-Large) on large databases (ImageNet, Places365) is effective for distinguishing melanoma from benign nevi with reduced training time compared to training from scratch.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. benign nevus), excluding other skin lesion types present in the ISIC database (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma).
The dataset selection from PH2 excluded atypical nevi, using only common nevi and melanomas, which may limit the system's generalizability to atypical presentations.
The sample sizes are small (100 images for PH2, 200 for ISIC 2019), which may lead to overfitting or biased performance estimates compared to larger-scale studies.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as point estimates of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and Dice coefficient.

The fusion weights were determined based on validation accuracy, which introduces a potential data leakage risk if the validation set is not strictly held out from the training process or if the weights are optimized on the same data used for final evaluation.

Preprocessing limitations were noted: the DullRazor hair removal algorithm was not always successful, particularly for thick dark hair covering the lesion or very thin hairs, and it did not remove dermatologist markers or water drops.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets outside of PH2 and ISIC 2019, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness.

The custom neural network architecture required significantly different complexity for different datasets (72 hidden layers for PH2 vs. 400 for ISIC 2019), suggesting a lack of a unified, scalable architecture without manual tuning.

The hardware specifications (Intel Core i7, 16 GB RAM) are modest for deep learning, and training times varied significantly (e.g., NasNet-Large took ~749 minutes), which may impact deployment feasibility in resource-constrained settings.
